title: Unit 1: Fundamentals of Cyber Security
description: The bedrock of digital safety. Understand the 'Why' and 'How' of cybersecurity.

modules.title: Introduction to Cyber Security
modules.description: Foundational concepts, the CIA triad, and why everyone is a target.

cards.title: What is Cyber Security?
cards.body: It is the art of protecting networks, devices, and data from unauthorized access or criminal use. In the physical world, we have locks and guards; in the digital world, we have cybersecurity.

cards.title: The Scale of the Problem
cards.body: Every 39 seconds, a hacker attack occurs somewhere in the world. With billions of devices connected, the 'attack surface' is practically infinite. Your phone is a doorway into your entire life.

quiz.question: What is the primary goal of cybersecurity?
options: To protect systems and data from digital attacks
quiz.explanation: Cybersecurity focuses on defending computers, servers, mobile devices, electronic systems, networks, and data from malicious attacks.

cards.title: Confidentiality
cards.body: Keeping information private. Only authorized people should access it. Tools: Strong passwords, Encryption, and 2FA. Example: Your email should only be accessible by you.

cards.title: Integrity
cards.body: Ensuring data is correct and untampered. Data should not be changed by unauthorized users. Example: Your exam marks or bank balance should not be altered by hackers.

cards.title: Availability
cards.body: Systems and data must be accessible when needed. Tools: Backups and maintenance. Example: Online banking and ATMs should be available 24/7.

quiz.question: Which pillar of the CIA Triad is violated if a hacker changes the grades in a university database?
quiz.explanation: Integrity ensures data is accurate and untampered. Changing data without authorization is a direct violation of integrity.

cards.title: Financial Impact
cards.body: Cybercrime is expected to cost the world $10.5 trillion annually by 2025. This includes stolen money, lost productivity, and the high cost of repairing damaged systems.

cards.title: Reputational Damage
cards.body: For a business, a data breach means a loss of customer trust. 60% of small companies go out of business within 6 months of a major cyber attack.

quiz.question: What percentage of small businesses fail after a major cyber attack?
quiz.explanation: The financial and reputational blow of a hack is often too much for small businesses to recover from, leading to 60% closing down.

cards.title: Privacy
cards.body: The right to control your own information. It's about 'Who has access' and 'What are they doing with it?'

cards.title: Security
cards.body: The protection of information. It's the 'Lock on the door'. You can have security without privacy (e.g., a prison), but you can't have privacy without security.

quiz.question: Can you have true digital privacy without security?
options: No, security is required to protect privacy
quiz.explanation: Security provides the technical barriers that keep your private data safe from unauthorized access.

cards.title: The Weakest Link
cards.body: 95% of cybersecurity breaches are caused by human error. This includes clicking on bad links, using '123456' as a password, or leaving a laptop unlocked.

cards.title: Social Engineering
cards.body: Hackers 'Hack People' before they hack computers. They use psychology to trick you into giving up secrets. This is often more effective than technical hacking.

quiz.question: What is the primary cause of 95% of cybersecurity breaches?
quiz.explanation: Humans are often targeted because we are prone to mistakes, curiosity, and emotional manipulation (Social Engineering).

modules.title: Types of Cyber Security
modules.description: From networks to your pocket: the different domains of protection.

cards.title: Network Security
cards.body: Protects internet connections. Tools: Firewalls (digital borders), VPNs (secure tunnels), and IDS (Intrusion Detection Systems).

cards.title: Cloud Security
cards.body: Protects data on servers like Google or Amazon. Shared Responsibility: The provider secures the 'Cloud', but YOU must secure your 'Account'.

quiz.question: Who is responsible for securing your files in Google Drive?
quiz.explanation: This is the 'Shared Responsibility Model'. Google secures the infrastructure, but you must secure your account with a strong password and MFA.

cards.title: App Security
cards.body: Ensures apps (like CyberShield) are built safely. Developers must check for 'Bugs' and 'Vulnerabilities' during the coding process.

cards.title: Data Security
cards.body: Protecting the data itself. Encryption 'wraps' data in a secret code. Access control 'locks' it so only the owner can open it.

quiz.question: What is the process of making data unreadable to everyone except the owner?
quiz.explanation: Encryption is the primary method for protecting data confidentiality by scrambling it into unreadable cipher text.

cards.title: Endpoint Security
cards.body: Endpoints are user devices: Phones, Laptops, and Tablets. They are the 'Edge' of the network and the most common entry point for malware.

cards.title: IoT: The Internet of Things
cards.body: Smart TVs, Watches, and Fridges. These often have weak security. Hackers can use a smart lightbulb as a bridge to get into your main home Wi-Fi.

quiz.question: Which of these is considered an 'Endpoint'?
quiz.explanation: Endpoints are the devices at the end of a connection that users interact with directly, like laptops and phones.

cards.title: App Permissions
cards.body: Does a Calculator app need your GPS location? No. Always review permissions. 'Over-permissioned' apps are often spyware in disguise.

cards.title: Juice Jacking
cards.body: Beware of public USB charging ports (Airports/Cafes). Data can be stolen from your phone while it charges. Use a 'Data Blocker' or a wall outlet instead.

quiz.question: What is 'Juice Jacking'?
options: Malware spread via public USB charging ports
quiz.explanation: Juice jacking is a security exploit where an attacker uses a charging port to install malware or copy sensitive data from a mobile device.

cards.title: National Safety
cards.body: Cybersecurity isn't just for computers. It protects the systems that provide Water, Electricity, and Hospital services for entire cities.

cards.title: SCADA Systems
cards.body: Special systems (SCADA) control factories and power plants. If hacked, they can cause physical damage, like making a generator explode or poisoning water.

quiz.question: What kind of systems control power plants and factories?
quiz.explanation: SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) systems are used to monitor and control industrial processes and infrastructure.

modules.title: Hackers & Ethics
modules.description: Understanding the mindset: the good, the bad, and the grey.

cards.title: White Hat
cards.body: Ethical Hackers. They have permission and use their skills to find and fix holes. They help protect the internet.

cards.title: Black Hat
cards.body: Cybercriminals. They hack for profit, revenge, or political gain. They cause damage and steal data.

cards.title: Grey Hat
cards.body: They have no permission, but no bad intent. They might hack a company and then tell them about the bug, hoping for a reward.

quiz.question: Which hacker has the owner's permission to test a system?
quiz.explanation: White Hat hackers are authorized professionals who help organizations secure their systems.

cards.title: Money (The #1 Driver)
cards.body: Most hackers want profit. Stolen credit cards, Ransomware, and Selling corporate secrets on the Dark Web are highly lucrative.

cards.title: Hacktivism
cards.body: Hacking for a cause. These hackers target organizations they disagree with to send a message or leak embarrassing info (e.g., Anonymous).

quiz.question: What is a 'Hacktivist'?
options: A hacker who works for a bank
A hacker motivated by social or political causes
A hacker who only uses Mac computers
quiz.explanation: Hacktivism is the use of hacking techniques to promote a political agenda or social change.

cards.title: Script Kiddies
cards.body: Non-experts who use 'Scripts' or tools made by others. They don't know how they work; they just want to cause chaos.

cards.title: Nation-State Hackers
cards.body: The most dangerous. They are funded by governments to spy on other countries or disrupt their infrastructure. They have unlimited time and money.

quiz.question: Which type of hacker has the most resources and funding?
quiz.explanation: Nation-state hackers are government-backed, giving them access to massive funding and advanced technology.

cards.title: Hacking for Profit
cards.body: Companies like Google and Meta pay Ethical Hackers thousands of dollars to find bugs in their apps. This is a legal and high-paying career path.

cards.title: Responsible Disclosure
cards.body: Instead of leaking a bug, ethical hackers report it privately and give the company time to fix it. Only then do they talk about it publicly.

quiz.question: What is a 'Bug Bounty'?
options: A reward for finding and reporting a security bug
quiz.explanation: Bug bounties encourage ethical hackers to help companies secure their products in exchange for money or recognition.

cards.title: Anonymous
cards.body: A decentralized group known for hacktivism. They wear Guy Fawkes masks and target governments or corporations they believe are behaving unethically.

cards.title: Lazarus Group
cards.body: A notorious group linked to North Korea. They are responsible for the Sony Pictures hack and the theft of $81 million from a Bangladesh bank.

quiz.question: Which group is famous for using 'Guy Fawkes' masks?
quiz.explanation: The mask is a symbol of the Anonymous collective, used in both digital and physical protests.

modules.title: Why Students are Targets
modules.description: The 'Perfect Storm' of access and inexperience.

cards.title: High Value Access
cards.body: Student accounts grant access to University Wi-Fi, VPNs, and massive research databases. A hacker using YOUR account looks like a legitimate student.

cards.title: PII: Identity Gold
cards.body: Student records contain everything needed for identity theft: Full names, Home addresses, Parents' info, and SSNs/ID numbers.

quiz.question: Why would a hacker want a student's university login?
options: To gain access to the university's internal network
quiz.explanation: A student account is a 'foot in the door' to a much larger, more valuable corporate or academic network.

cards.title: The Trap of 'Free'
cards.body: Pirated software (Cracks, Torrents) often has 'Trojan' viruses hidden inside. You think you're getting a free game, but you're giving a hacker control of your PC.

cards.title: Illegal Streaming
cards.body: Websites that offer 'Free Movies' use malicious ads. One wrong click can install a keylogger that records everything you type, including passwords.

quiz.question: What is often hidden inside 'cracked' or pirated software?
quiz.explanation: Hackers provide 'free' software as a lure to infect users who are trying to bypass paying for apps or games.

cards.title: Oversharing
cards.body: Posting a photo of your new ID card or 'First Day of School' with your location tag gives hackers all the info they need for a targeted scam.

cards.title: Spear Phishing
cards.body: Attackers look at your LinkedIn or Insta to see where you study and who your friends are. They send a fake email that looks 100% real because it uses your personal info.

quiz.question: What is 'Spear Phishing'?
options: A phishing attack using a literal spear
A generic email sent to millions
A highly targeted phishing attack using personal details
quiz.explanation: Spear phishing is more dangerous because it's personalized, making it much harder to detect than a generic scam email.

cards.title: False Confidence
cards.body: Being 'good with computers' doesn't make you safe. Even tech experts are tricked by sophisticated social engineering and 'Zero-Day' exploits.

cards.title: Public Wi-Fi Risks
cards.body: Students often use cafe or library Wi-Fi. Without a VPN, an attacker in the same cafe can 'Sniff' your traffic and steal your session cookies.

quiz.question: Which tool should students use to stay safe on public Wi-Fi?
quiz.explanation: A VPN creates an encrypted tunnel for your data, protecting it from anyone else on the same public Wi-Fi network.

cards.title: Zero Unemployment
cards.body: There is a massive shortage of cybersecurity pros. Jobs include SOC Analyst (Defenders), Pentesters (Attackers), and Forensics (Detectives).

cards.title: Where to Start?
cards.body: Learn the basics (like this app!), get certifications (CompTIA Security+), and join 'Capture the Flag' (CTF) competitions to practice.

quiz.question: What is a 'CTF' in cybersecurity learning?
quiz.explanation: CTFs are competitions where students solve security puzzles to find 'Flags', helping them learn real-world hacking skills safely.

modules.title: Real-life Case Studies
modules.description: Learning from history's biggest cyber crimes.

cards.title: Global Chaos
cards.body: In 2017, a virus called WannaCry infected 200,000+ PCs in 150 countries. It locked files and demanded Bitcoin. It paralyzed hospitals in the UK.

cards.title: The Lesson
cards.body: The attack spread through a Windows bug that had ALREADY been patched. The victim companies simply hadn't clicked 'Update'. Always update your OS!

quiz.question: What was the main way to have prevented the WannaCry infection?
quiz.explanation: WannaCry targeted a vulnerability for which a security patch was already available; updating the system would have blocked it.

cards.title: Supply Chain Attack
cards.body: Hackers didn't attack the government directly. Instead, they hacked a software company called SolarWinds and hid malware inside a 'Software Update'.

cards.title: Invisible Entry
cards.body: Thousands of companies and US government agencies installed the 'Update' thinking it was safe. It was one of the most sophisticated spying operations in history.

quiz.question: What is a 'Supply Chain' attack?
options: Infecting a third-party software that many others use
quiz.explanation: By hacking one supplier, attackers can gain access to all of that supplier's customers at once.

cards.title: Digital Weapon
cards.body: Stuxnet was a worm designed to target nuclear centrifuges in Iran. It was the first time a cyber attack caused significant physical damage in the real world.

cards.title: Air-Gapped Bypass
cards.body: The centrifuges weren't on the internet. Stuxnet spread via infected USB drives carried in by unsuspecting employees. This is why USB security is vital.

quiz.question: How did Stuxnet get into the isolated nuclear facility?
quiz.explanation: Even 'Air-Gapped' systems (not connected to internet) can be hacked through physical media like USB sticks.

cards.title: Billions of Victims
cards.body: Yahoo suffered breaches that exposed data of 3 BILLION accounts. Names, emails, and 'Hashed' passwords were stolen.

cards.title: Financial Fallout
cards.body: Because they didn't disclose the hack for years, Yahoo's value dropped by $350 million when they tried to sell the company to Verizon.

quiz.question: How many Yahoo accounts were affected in their largest breach?
quiz.explanation: It remains one of the largest data breaches in history, affecting every single user account Yahoo had at the time.

cards.title: Social Engineering
cards.body: A small district in India became the center of a massive phishing scam. Scammers would call people pretending to be bank officials to steal OTPs.

cards.title: No Tech Required
cards.body: They didn't use fancy code. They used greed and fear to trick people. This shows that 'Vishing' (Voice Phishing) is just as dangerous as malware.

quiz.question: What is 'Vishing'?
quiz.explanation: Vishing is social engineering via phone calls, often used to create a sense of urgency to steal sensitive bank details.

title: Unit 2: Linux Basics for Cyber Security
description: Mastering the preferred operating system of security professionals.

modules.title: OS Fundamentals & Linux Importance
modules.description: The foundation of computing and why Linux rules the security world.

cards.title: The Intermediary
cards.body: An OS acts as a bridge between the user and the computer hardware. It manages resources like CPU, memory, and storage, making it easier to run applications.

cards.title: Core Functions
cards.body: The OS handles: 1. Security (Firewalls), 2. Memory Management, 3. Process Management (Multitasking), and 4. Device/File Management.

cards.title: Types of OS
cards.body: Modern systems include Multi-user (Linux/Windows), Real-Time (Robotics/Medical), and Mobile (Android/iOS). Each is optimized for specific tasks.

quiz.question: Which type of OS is designed to execute tasks within a guaranteed, strict time limit?
quiz.explanation: RTOS is used in mission-critical systems like medical devices and air traffic control where timing is everything.

cards.title: Open Source Transparency
cards.body: Since the source code is public, security experts can audit it for backdoors. Patches for vulnerabilities are often released within days, not months.

cards.title: Granular Permissions
cards.body: Linux follows a strict permission model (Owner/Group/Others). Malware can't easily install itself system-wide because system files require 'Root' (Sudo) access.

cards.title: Native Security Tools
cards.body: Over 90% of security tools (Nmap, Wireshark, Metasploit) are Linux-native. They require low-level system access that Windows often restricts.

quiz.question: Why is 'Open Source' considered a security advantage for Linux?
options: Anyone can audit the code for vulnerabilities
quiz.explanation: Transparency allows the global security community to find and fix bugs much faster than proprietary software companies.

cards.title: The Kernel
cards.body: The Kernel is the heart of Linux. It talks directly to the hardware. As a user, you never touch it directly; instead, you talk to the 'Shell'.

cards.title: The Shell (CLI)
cards.body: The Shell is a program that takes your commands and gives them to the Kernel. The most common shell is 'Bash'. It is much more powerful than a Graphical UI (GUI).

quiz.question: What is the relationship between the Shell and the Kernel?
options: The Shell translates user commands for the Kernel
The Kernel is a web browser
quiz.explanation: The Shell acts as the command-line interface that allows users to interact with the Kernel's functions.

cards.title: Many Flavors
cards.body: Because Linux is open, anyone can bundle the Kernel with different tools. These bundles are called 'Distributions'. Examples: Ubuntu (Easy), Debian (Stable), Kali (Security).

cards.title: Choosing a Distro
cards.body: For servers, CentOS or Debian is common. For hacking, Kali or Parrot OS is used. For daily use, Linux Mint or Ubuntu is preferred.

quiz.question: What is a Linux 'Distribution'?
options: A bundle of the Linux Kernel plus specific software and tools
quiz.explanation: Distros are customized versions of Linux tailored for specific needs, like security testing or general office work.

cards.title: Free as in Freedom
cards.body: Linux is licensed under the GPL. This means you are free to run, study, share, and modify the software. This 'Crowdsourced' development makes it highly robust.

cards.title: Community Power
cards.body: Thousands of developers worldwide contribute to Linux. If a major bug is found, it's often fixed by the community before the official 'company' even acknowledges it.

quiz.question: What does 'Free Software' mean in the context of Linux?
options: Freedom to use, modify, and distribute the code
quiz.explanation: Open source is about freedom (Libre), not just price. It allows anyone to improve the system.

modules.title: Kali Linux Overview
modules.description: Introduction to the industry-standard OS for penetration testing.

cards.title: What is Kali Linux?
cards.body: Maintained by Offensive Security, it's a Debian-based distribution pre-loaded with hundreds of tools for pentesting, forensics, and reverse engineering.

cards.title: Key Features
cards.body: 1. 600+ Security Tools. 2. Custom Kernel (supports Wi-Fi injection). 3. 'Undercover Mode' to look like Windows. 4. Live Boot (runs from USB without trace).

quiz.question: What is the primary purpose of 'Kali Undercover' mode?
options: To make the desktop look like Windows
quiz.explanation: Undercover mode helps security pros blend in when working in public places by mimicking a standard Windows environment.

cards.title: Virtual Machines (VM)
cards.body: The safest way to learn. You run Kali 'inside' Windows or Mac using VirtualBox. If you break Kali, your main computer stays safe.

cards.title: Live USB with Persistence
cards.body: You can boot Kali from a USB drive. 'Persistence' allows you to save your files and settings on the USB so they are there next time you plug it in.

quiz.question: What is the main benefit of using a Virtual Machine for Kali?
options: It's faster than a real PC
It isolates Kali from your main operating system for safety
quiz.explanation: VMs provide a 'Sandbox' environment where you can experiment without risking your primary OS or data.

cards.title: Information Gathering
cards.body: Tools like Nmap and Maltego. Used to 'Scout' a target before attacking. You find open ports, services, and hidden info.

cards.title: Exploitation Tools
cards.body: The most famous is Metasploit. These tools are used to actually 'Break In' once a vulnerability is found in the scouting phase.

quiz.question: Which tool category is used to find open ports on a server?
quiz.explanation: Information gathering (or reconnaissance) is the first step where you map out the target's defenses.

cards.title: ProxyChains
cards.body: A tool that hides your IP by bouncing your connection through multiple servers (Proxies) before it hits the target. Like a digital trail of breadcrumbs.

cards.title: MAC Changer
cards.body: Every Wi-Fi card has a unique 'MAC Address'. Kali lets you change this to a random one so your physical device cannot be tracked on a network.

quiz.question: Why would a hacker use a MAC Changer?
options: To hide their physical device's hardware ID
quiz.explanation: Changing your MAC address makes it harder for network admins to identify or block your specific laptop/device.

modules.title: Linux File System & Navigation
modules.description: Learning the tree structure and the power of the command line.

cards.title: Everything is a File
cards.body: In Linux, everything starts at '/' (Root). '/home' is for users, '/etc' for config files, and '/var/log' is where the system logs its activity.

cards.title: Important Folders
cards.body: 1. /bin: Basic commands (ls, cp). 2. /etc: Password/Network settings. 3. /var: Changing data (logs). 4. /root: The superuser's home.

quiz.question: Which directory contains system configuration files like network settings and passwords?
quiz.explanation: /etc is the central location for all system-wide configuration files in Linux.

cards.title: Navigating & Viewing
cards.body: 'ls' lists files. 'cd' changes directory. 'pwd' shows your current path. 'cat' prints the content of a file to your screen.

cards.title: Managing Files
cards.body: 'mkdir' creates folders. 'touch' creates empty files. 'cp' copies, 'mv' moves/renames, and 'rm' deletes. Use 'rm -r' to delete folders.

quiz.question: Which command tells you exactly what type of data a file contains (regardless of its extension)?
quiz.explanation: The 'file' command looks at the file's internal header to determine its true type, helping detect hidden malware.

cards.title: Searching with Grep
cards.body: 'grep' is a powerful search tool. Example: 'grep "password" log.txt' will find every line that contains the word password. Vital for finding clues in huge files.

cards.title: Case Insensitivity
cards.body: Use 'grep -i' to ignore upper/lower case. Use 'grep -r' to search inside every file in a folder (recursive search).

quiz.question: What does the 'grep' command do?
options: Searches for a specific pattern of text within a file
quiz.explanation: Grep is the standard tool for searching text, making it essential for analyzing logs and source code.

cards.title: The Pipe (|)
cards.body: Piping takes the output of one command and gives it to another. Example: 'ls /etc | grep "net"' will list all files in /etc that have 'net' in their name.

cards.title: Output Redirection (>)
cards.body: Use '>' to save command output to a file instead of showing it on screen. 'ls > files.txt' creates a list of your files in a new text document.

quiz.question: Which symbol is used to 'pipe' output from one command to another?
quiz.explanation: The vertical bar (|) is the pipe symbol, allowing you to chain multiple simple commands into a powerful workflow.

cards.title: Locate
cards.body: The fastest way to find a file. It uses a pre-built database. Commands: 'updatedb' to refresh the list, and 'locate <filename>' to find it instantly.

cards.title: Find
cards.body: Slower but more powerful. It searches the live file system. You can find files by size, owner, or even when they were last modified (e.g., find files changed in the last 24h).

quiz.question: Which command is better for finding files based on their last modification time?
quiz.explanation: 'find' has many 'filters' (like -mtime) that allow for very specific searches beyond just the filename.

modules.title: Permissions & User Management
modules.description: Mastering access control and the power of the Root user.

cards.title: The Superuser (Root)
cards.body: Root has total control. It can delete anything and access any file. Standard users are restricted to their /home folder for safety.

cards.title: Sudo (Superuser Do)
cards.body: Instead of staying logged in as root (risky!), users use 'sudo' to run specific commands with root privileges. This is safer and logs every action.

quiz.question: What is the safest way to perform administrative tasks in Linux?
quiz.explanation: Sudo allows temporary elevated privileges without the extreme risk of staying logged in as the all-powerful Root user.

cards.title: Read, Write, Execute
cards.body: Permissions are set for Owner, Group, and Others. 'r' (read=4), 'w' (write=2), 'x' (execute=1). Together they form a 3-digit number (e.g., 755).

cards.title: Chmod & Chown
cards.body: 'chmod' changes file permissions (like chmod 700 for privacy). 'chown' changes the owner of a file. These are vital for securing data.

quiz.question: What does the numeric permission '755' mean?
options: Owner has full (7), Group/Others can Read/Execute (5)
No one can read the file
quiz.explanation: 7 (4+2+1) means rwx. 5 (4+1) means r-x. So Owner has full, others can read/run but not modify.

cards.title: What is SUID?
cards.body: Set User ID. When a file has SUID, it runs with the permissions of the OWNER, not the user who started it. Example: The 'passwd' command needs root power to change your password.

cards.title: Security Risk
cards.body: SUID is dangerous. If a hacker finds an exploit in a program with SUID (owned by root), they can instantly become root. Security auditors always search for 'Misconfigured SUID' files.

quiz.question: Why is a misconfigured SUID file a major security risk?
options: It allows a normal user to execute a program with Root privileges
quiz.explanation: SUID files are a common 'Privilege Escalation' vector used by hackers to gain full control of a system.

cards.title: User Groups
cards.body: Groups allow you to give permissions to many users at once. Common groups: 'sudo' (admins), 'www-data' (web servers), 'students'.

cards.title: The Sudoers File
cards.body: Located at /etc/sudoers. This file lists exactly which users are allowed to use the 'sudo' command. NEVER edit this with a normal editor; always use 'visudo' to prevent errors.

quiz.question: What is the specialized tool used to edit the Sudoers file safely?
quiz.explanation: visudo checks your syntax before saving. A single typo in the sudoers file can lock everyone (even admins) out of the system.

modules.title: Software & System Logs
modules.description: Installing tools and tracking every move on the system.

cards.title: What is APT?
cards.body: Advanced Package Tool. It handles installing and updating software from trusted 'Repositories' (online app stores for Linux).

cards.title: Core Commands
cards.body: 'sudo apt update' refreshes the list. 'sudo apt install <name>' adds software. 'sudo apt upgrade' installs latest security patches.

quiz.question: Which command should you run first before installing any new software via APT?
quiz.explanation: 'apt update' synchronizes your local list of packages with the online servers to ensure you get the latest version.

cards.title: The Digital Trail
cards.body: Logs are the history of the system. Found in /var/log/. 'auth.log' tracks login attempts. 'syslog' records general events.

cards.title: Live Monitoring
cards.body: 'tail -f /var/log/auth.log' shows login attempts as they happen. This is how security pros spot brute-force attacks in real-time.

quiz.question: Which log file is most important for investigating unauthorized login attempts?
quiz.explanation: auth.log (or secure log in some systems) tracks every authentication attempt, success or failure.

cards.title: What are Services?
cards.body: Services (or Daemons) are programs that run in the background without a window. Examples: Web servers (Apache), SSH for remote access, and Firewalls.

cards.title: Systemctl
cards.body: The main tool to manage services. 'systemctl start' turns a service on. 'systemctl stop' turns it off. 'systemctl status' tells you if it's running correctly.

quiz.question: Which command would you use to check if the Firewall service is currently running?
quiz.explanation: The 'status' command provides a detailed report on whether a service is active, inactive, or has crashed.

cards.title: IP & Interfaces
cards.body: The 'ip a' command shows your network interfaces (like eth0 for cable or wlan0 for Wi-Fi) and your IP address. 'ping' tests if you can reach another computer.

cards.title: Netstat & Ports
cards.body: 'netstat -tunlp' shows every 'Door' (Port) that is open on your computer. If you see a port you don't recognize, it could be a hacker's backdoor.

quiz.question: Which command helps you identify which ports are 'Listening' for connections on your machine?
quiz.explanation: Netstat (Network Statistics) is vital for identifying unauthorized network services running on your system.

title: Unit 3: Security Concepts & Principles
description: Deep dive into the CIA Triad and the pillars of digital trust.

modules.title: Confidentiality & Access Control
modules.description: Ensuring only authorized eyes see your data.

cards.title: The First Pillar
cards.body: Confidentiality is about ensuring that sensitive information is not disclosed to unauthorized individuals. It is the 'Need to Know' principle in action.

cards.title: Real-world Example
cards.body: A hospital employee looking up a celebrity’s medical records without authorization is a direct breach of confidentiality, even if they don't change anything.

quiz.question: What is the core goal of Confidentiality?
options: To make sure data is correct
To prevent unauthorized disclosure of data
To ensure the server never goes down
quiz.explanation: Confidentiality is exclusively about restricting access to sensitive information to only those who are authorized.

cards.title: DAC: Discretionary
cards.body: The owner of the file decides who gets access. It's the most flexible but least secure model. Common in personal computers (e.g., sharing a Google Doc).

cards.title: MAC: Mandatory
cards.body: The system enforces access based on security labels (like 'Top Secret'). Users cannot change these permissions. Used in high-security government environments.

cards.title: RBAC: Role-Based
cards.body: Permissions are assigned to 'Roles' (like Manager, HR, Student). Users get access based on their job function. This is the standard for most businesses.

quiz.question: Which access control model is based on an individual's position or job function?
quiz.explanation: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) simplifies management by grouping permissions into roles rather than assigning them to every individual.

cards.title: Granular Control
cards.body: Modern operating systems allow specific permissions: Read (see), Write (change), and Execute (run). A nurse might 'Read' a file, but only a Doctor can 'Write' to it.

cards.title: Inheritance
cards.body: Permissions usually flow down. If you restrict a folder, all files inside it are automatically restricted too. This prevents accidental data exposure.

quiz.question: If a user has 'Read-Only' permission, can they save changes to that file?
options: No, they can only view the content
quiz.explanation: Read-Only permission strictly prevents any modifications to the original file content.

cards.title: Encryption at Rest
cards.body: Protects data while it's sitting on a hard drive or USB. If the device is stolen, the thief can't read the files without the key (e.g., BitLocker or FileVault).

cards.title: Encryption in Transit
cards.body: Protects data as it travels across the internet (Wi-Fi, Fiber). HTTPS/TLS ensures that 'Sniffers' on public Wi-Fi can't steal your login details.

quiz.question: Which type of encryption protects your data if your laptop is physically stolen?
quiz.explanation: Encryption at Rest ensures that the data on the physical storage device remains unreadable without the decryption key.

cards.title: Hiding in Plain Sight
cards.body: Data masking replaces sensitive info with a non-sensitive version. Example: Seeing 'XXXX-XXXX-1234' instead of your full credit card number.

cards.title: Anonymization
cards.body: Removing identifying details (name, SSN) from a dataset so it can be used for research without exposing people's privacy.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of showing 'XXXX' for part of a phone number in an app?
options: To hide the full number from unauthorized eyes (Data Masking)
To make it easier to remember
quiz.explanation: This is a form of data masking, a confidentiality mechanism that shows only the necessary part of a sensitive data field.

modules.title: Integrity & Availability
modules.description: Keeping data accurate and services running.

cards.title: Data Accuracy
cards.body: Integrity ensures data is accurate, complete, and hasn't been tampered with. If a hacker changes your bank balance from $10 to $1,000,000, integrity is lost.

cards.title: Authorized Changes Only
cards.body: Data should only be modified by authorized people in authorized ways. This includes preventing accidental corruption during a power cut.

quiz.question: What is the main goal of Integrity?
options: To ensure data is correct and untampered
To make data easy to delete
quiz.explanation: Integrity is about the trustworthiness of the data—making sure it hasn't been changed maliciously or accidentally.

cards.title: Digital Fingerprints
cards.body: A Hash (like SHA-256) takes data and turns it into a fixed string. If you change even one comma in a 500-page book, the hash will change completely.

cards.title: Verification
cards.body: Software downloads often provide a 'Checksum'. You run a hash tool on your PC; if your hash matches the website's hash, the file is safe and original.

quiz.question: Which of these acts like a 'digital fingerprint' for a file?
quiz.explanation: A hash is a unique mathematical output that represents a specific set of data. It changes if the data changes.

cards.title: Beyond Hashing
cards.body: A digital signature combines a hash with the sender's private key. This proves exactly who sent the message AND that it wasn't tampered with.

cards.title: Software Trust
cards.body: Microsoft and Apple 'Sign' their updates. Your computer verifies this signature before installing to ensure a hacker didn't swap the update with malware.

quiz.question: What two things does a Digital Signature prove?
quiz.explanation: Digital signatures provide both authenticity (who sent it) and integrity (it hasn't been changed).

cards.title: The Third Pillar
cards.body: Availability means systems and data are ready when needed. If a bank's ATM network is down for 5 hours, they have a major availability failure.

cards.title: Redundancy
cards.body: Redundancy means having spares. RAID (duplicate hard drives), Load Balancers (multiple servers), and Dual Power Supplies prevent single points of failure.

quiz.question: What is 'Redundancy' in a data center?
options: Having backup/extra systems to prevent downtime
quiz.explanation: Redundancy ensures that if one component fails, another is ready to take over instantly, maintaining service availability.

cards.title: The 3-2-1 Rule
cards.body: 3 copies of data, on 2 different media (e.g., Disk and Cloud), with 1 copy kept Offsite. This is the gold standard for personal and business backups.

cards.title: Disaster Recovery
cards.body: A plan to restore everything after a major event like a fire, flood, or massive Ransomware attack. It includes 'RTO' (how fast we must recover).

quiz.question: What does the '1' in the 3-2-1 backup rule stand for?
quiz.explanation: Keeping one copy offsite (away from your physical location) protects your data even if the building is destroyed.

cards.title: DDoS Attacks
cards.body: Distributed Denial of Service. Thousands of 'Zombie' computers flood a website with junk traffic until it crashes, making it unavailable to real users.

cards.title: Defenses
cards.body: Rate Limiting (blocking too many requests), WAF (Web Application Firewalls), and CDNs (Cloudflare) help filter bad traffic and keep sites online.

quiz.question: What is the main purpose of a DDoS attack?
options: To crash a website and make it unavailable
quiz.explanation: DDoS is a direct attack on Availability, designed to overwhelm a server so it can't serve legitimate users.

modules.title: Advanced Authentication
modules.description: Modern ways to prove who you are.

cards.title: Something You Know
cards.body: The most common factor. Includes Passwords, PINs, and security questions. Weakest on its own because secrets can be guessed or stolen.

cards.title: Something You Have
cards.body: Physical items like your Smartphone (for OTPs), a Smart Card, or a Hardware Key (YubiKey). Harder to steal remotely.

cards.title: Something You Are
cards.body: Biometrics: Fingerprints, Face ID, or Retina scans. Very convenient, but difficult to change if the biometric data is leaked.

quiz.question: Which factor of authentication is a 'Fingerprint'?
quiz.explanation: Biometrics like fingerprints are physical characteristics—something you inherently ARE.

cards.title: Why MFA?
cards.body: Multi-Factor Authentication requires 2 or more DIFFERENT factors. If a hacker steals your password (Know), they still can't get in without your phone (Have).

cards.title: SMS vs. App
cards.body: SMS codes are common but 'Weak' (SIM Swapping risk). Authenticator Apps (Google, Microsoft) are 'Stronger'. Hardware keys are the 'Best' (Phishing-proof).

quiz.question: Why is MFA considered so effective?
options: It requires an attacker to compromise two different types of security
quiz.explanation: By requiring two different factors (like a password and a physical token), MFA stops 99% of automated account hacks.

cards.title: Convenience vs Risk
cards.body: Biometrics (FaceID/Fingerprint) are fast and can't be forgotten. However, unlike a password, you can't 'Reset' your face if it's spoofed by a high-res photo or 3D mask.

cards.title: Secure Enclave
cards.body: Modern phones don't store your actual fingerprint image. They store a mathematical 'Representation' in a separate, isolated chip that even the OS can't touch.

quiz.question: Where is biometric data typically stored on a modern smartphone?
options: In a text file on the SD card
quiz.explanation: A Secure Enclave or Trusted Execution Environment (TEE) is an isolated hardware area that keeps sensitive biometrics safe.

cards.title: One Key, many Doors
cards.body: SSO allows you to log in once (e.g., with Google or Okta) and automatically access Slack, Zoom, and HR tools without re-typing credentials.

cards.title: The Risks
cards.body: SSO reduces 'Password Fatigue', but creates a 'Single Point of Failure'. If your main Google account is hacked, the hacker has access to ALL connected apps.

quiz.question: What is 'Password Fatigue'?
options: The stress of remembering dozens of different passwords
quiz.explanation: Password fatigue leads users to reuse weak passwords. SSO helps solve this by using one strong login for everything.

cards.title: The Turing Test
cards.body: CAPTCHAs verify that the user is a human. This prevents bots from creating 1 million fake accounts or guessing thousands of passwords per second.

cards.title: Modern Evolution
cards.body: Old CAPTCHAs asked you to read blurry text. New 'Invisible' versions (reCAPTCHA v3) track mouse behavior and typing speed to detect bots silently.

quiz.question: What is the primary job of a CAPTCHA?
options: To distinguish between a human user and an automated bot
To speed up the login process
quiz.explanation: CAPTCHA is a 'Gatekeeper' that blocks automated attacks (bots) while allowing legitimate humans through.

cards.title: Unusual Sign-in Alerts
cards.body: If you log in from India and then 5 minutes later from London, the system flags 'Impossible Travel' and blocks access or sends an alert.

cards.title: Step-up Auth
cards.body: The system might not ask for MFA every time, but if you try to transfer $10,000, it 'Steps up' and demands a fingerprint or OTP for that specific action.

quiz.question: What is 'Impossible Travel' in cybersecurity monitoring?
options: Login attempts from two distant locations in a very short time
quiz.explanation: It's a risk signal. If your account is accessed from different countries too quickly, it's a sign of a shared or stolen credential.

modules.title: The Art of Non-Repudiation
modules.description: Making it impossible to deny an action.

cards.title: Digital Accountability
cards.body: Non-repudiation means a person cannot deny their actions. If you sign a $1M contract digitally, you can't later say 'It wasn't me' if the system is secure.

cards.title: The Three Pillars
cards.body: It relies on: 1. Authentic Identity (PKI), 2. Integrity (Data didn't change), and 3. Time (Trusted timestamps).

quiz.question: In a digital world, what does 'Repudiation' mean?
options: Denying that you performed an action or sent a message
quiz.explanation: Repudiation is the act of denying involvement. Non-repudiation prevents this denial.

cards.title: Public Key Infrastructure
cards.body: PKI is the backbone of trust on the internet. It uses 'Public Keys' (shared with everyone) and 'Private Keys' (kept secret by the owner) to sign data.

cards.title: The Trust Chain
cards.body: Just like a passport is trusted because the government issued it, a Digital Certificate is trusted because a 'Certificate Authority' (CA) signed it.

quiz.question: Which key is used to create a digital signature for non-repudiation?
quiz.explanation: The Private Key must be kept secret and only used by the owner to create signatures that others can then verify with the Public Key.

cards.title: Your Digital ID
cards.body: A certificate contains your Public Key and identity info. It is signed by a CA (like DigiCert). When you visit a website, the 'Padlock' icon shows its certificate is valid.

cards.title: Revocation (CRL/OCSP)
cards.body: If a private key is stolen, the certificate must be 'Revoked' (cancelled). Browsers check a Revocation List to make sure a certificate is still trustworthy.

quiz.question: What happens if a digital certificate is 'Revoked'?
options: It is no longer trusted by browsers and systems
quiz.explanation: Revocation is the process of declaring a certificate invalid before its official expiry date, usually due to security compromise.

cards.title: The Paperless Trail
cards.body: High-security systems record every single action in an 'Audit Log'. These logs must be 'Immutable'—meaning even an admin cannot delete or change them.

cards.title: Log Chaining
cards.body: Modern logs use 'Hashing' to link each entry to the previous one. If you delete entry #5, the 'Chain' breaks for all subsequent entries, exposing the tampering.

quiz.question: Why must audit logs be 'Immutable'?
options: To prevent someone from erasing evidence of their actions
To make them easier to read
To share them on social media
quiz.explanation: Immutable logs provide a reliable history that can be used in court or forensics to prove what actually happened.

cards.title: E-Sign vs Digital Signature
cards.body: An 'E-Sign' is broad (like typing your name). A 'Digital Signature' is a cryptographic proof. Many countries (like India under the IT Act 2000) give them full legal weight.

cards.title: QES: Qualified Signatures
cards.body: The highest legal level. Requires a hardware device (like a Smart Card or USB Token) and face-to-face identity verification. Equivalent to a handwritten signature.

quiz.question: Is a digital signature legally binding in India?
options: No, only paper signatures are valid
quiz.explanation: India's Information Technology Act 2000 provides legal recognition for electronic signatures and records.

cards.title: Procedural Proof
cards.body: Order confirmations and 'Password Changed' emails act as lightweight non-repudiation. If you don't dispute an order email immediately, it serves as evidence of consent.

cards.title: DKIM & SPF
cards.body: These technical headers prove an email actually came from Amazon or Google and wasn't 'Spoofed' by a hacker. They add a layer of trust to everyday emails.

quiz.question: What is the primary value of an order confirmation email in a dispute?
options: It provides a timestamped record of the transaction details
It proves your internet is working
quiz.explanation: Confirmation emails serve as procedural evidence that a specific event occurred at a specific time, sent to a registered account.

title: Unit 4: Ethical Hacking Phases
description: The methodology of a professional security auditor.

modules.title: Reconnaissance (Footprinting)
modules.description: Gathering information about the target without their knowledge.

cards.title: Passive Reconnaissance
cards.body: Gathering info without interacting with the target. Examples: Searching LinkedIn, Google, or public records. The target has NO way of knowing they are being watched.

cards.title: Active Reconnaissance
cards.body: Directly interacting with the target's systems. Example: Pinging their server or visiting their website. This is riskier because the target might log your IP address.

quiz.question: Which type of reconnaissance is invisible to the target?
quiz.explanation: Passive recon uses third-party sources (like Google) so the target's own servers never see the attacker's connection.

cards.title: Advanced Search Operators
cards.body: Using special commands in Google to find hidden files. 'filetype:pdf' finds documents, 'site:nasa.gov' limits search to one domain, and 'intitle:index of' finds open server directories.

cards.title: The GHDB
cards.body: The Google Hacking Database (GHDB) is a list of famous 'Dorks' that find sensitive info like passwords, log files, and vulnerable webcams that were accidentally indexed by Google.

quiz.question: Which Google operator would you use to find only PDF files on a specific website?
quiz.explanation: The 'filetype:' operator is a powerful way to filter search results to specific document formats.

cards.title: WHOIS Records
cards.body: A public database that stores ownership info for every domain name. It can reveal the owner's name, email, phone number, and where the website is hosted.

cards.title: DNS Enumeration
cards.body: DNS is the 'Phonebook' of the internet. Tools like 'nslookup' or 'dig' reveal the IP addresses of a company's mail servers and internal subdomains (e.g., dev.company.com).

quiz.question: What information can a WHOIS lookup typically reveal?
quiz.explanation: WHOIS provides administrative and technical contact information for registered domain names.

cards.title: People as Targets
cards.body: Recon isn't just for computers. Hackers use LinkedIn to see who works in IT, what software they use (listed in job bios), and who the new, inexperienced employees are.

cards.title: The Power of Small Details
cards.body: Finding out a company uses 'Slack' or 'Outlook' allows a hacker to craft a fake login page that looks identical to the real thing, increasing the success of a phishing attack.

quiz.question: Why is LinkedIn a goldmine for ethical hackers doing recon?
options: To identify employees and the technologies they use
quiz.explanation: Employee profiles often list technical skills and tools used at work, giving hackers clues about the company's internal infrastructure.

cards.title: The Search Engine for Things
cards.body: Unlike Google, Shodan searches for 'Connected Devices'. It finds webcams, industrial control systems, routers, and smart fridges. It's often called the 'Scariest Search Engine'.

cards.title: Banner Grabbing
cards.body: Shodan works by 'Grabbing Banners'—the intro message a server sends when you connect. These banners often reveal the exact version of software, making it easy to find known exploits.

quiz.question: What is Shodan primarily used for?
options: Finding connected devices and IoT hardware
quiz.explanation: Shodan indexes everything connected to the internet that isn't a traditional website, from routers to power plant controllers.

modules.title: Scanning & Enumeration
modules.description: Mapping the network and finding open doors.

cards.title: The Network Mapper
cards.body: Nmap is the industry standard for network scanning. It sends packets to a target and analyzes the responses to see which 'Ports' (doors) are open.

cards.title: Basic Scan
cards.body: 'nmap 192.168.1.1' will check the 1,000 most common ports. If port 80 is open, there's a web server. If port 22 is open, there's a remote login (SSH) service.

quiz.question: What is an 'Open Port' in the context of scanning?
options: A service that is actively listening for incoming connections
quiz.explanation: An open port is like an unlocked door on a computer, indicating a service (like a website) is ready to talk to others.

cards.title: Service Detection (-sV)
cards.body: Standard scans just say 'Port 80 is open'. Using '-sV' forces Nmap to talk to the port to find out exactly what version of software is running (e.g., Apache 2.4.5).

cards.title: Stealth Scan (-sS)
cards.body: A 'SYN' scan. It starts a connection but cuts it off before it's finished. This is harder for simple firewalls to log than a full connection.

quiz.question: Which Nmap flag is used to detect the version of services running on open ports?
quiz.explanation: -sV (Service Version) is crucial for finding outdated software that might have known security holes.

cards.title: Identifying the OS (-O)
cards.body: Different Operating Systems (Windows, Linux, iOS) respond to network errors in slightly different ways. Nmap uses these 'Fingerprints' to guess the target's OS.

cards.title: Why it matters
cards.body: You don't want to use a Windows exploit on a Linux server. Knowing the OS allows the hacker to narrow down their attack strategy.

quiz.question: How does Nmap identify the Operating System of a target?
options: By analyzing small differences in how the target's network stack responds
By guessing based on the IP address
It reads a file on the desktop
quiz.explanation: This is called OS Fingerprinting; it uses the unique way each OS handles network traffic to identify it.

cards.title: Automated Scouting
cards.body: Tools like Nessus or OpenVAS go beyond Nmap. They have a database of thousands of 'Known Vulnerabilities' and check the target against all of them automatically.

cards.title: The Report
cards.body: These tools produce a list of 'Findings' ranked by severity (Low, Medium, High, Critical). Professional Pentesters use these reports to prioritize their work.

quiz.question: What is the primary difference between a Port Scanner (Nmap) and a Vulnerability Scanner (Nessus)?
options: Nessus actively looks for known security holes, not just open ports
quiz.explanation: Port scanners find 'doors', while vulnerability scanners check if those doors have 'broken locks' (vulnerabilities).

cards.title: What is Enumeration?
cards.body: The process of extracting user names, machine names, network resources, and shares. This is the 'Final Map' before the actual attack.

cards.title: Common Targets
cards.body: SMB (File sharing) and SNMP (Network management) are favorites. If misconfigured, they can leak every username on the network or even administrative passwords.

quiz.question: What is the main goal of the Enumeration phase?
options: To gather specific details like usernames and network shares
quiz.explanation: Enumeration is about creating a detailed list of assets and users to prepare for the exploitation phase.

modules.title: Gaining Access (Exploitation)
modules.description: Crossing the perimeter and entering the system.

cards.title: Exploitation Made Easy
cards.body: Metasploit is a massive library of 'Exploits' (the key) and 'Payloads' (the action). It automates the process of breaking into a system.

cards.title: How it works
cards.body: 1. Choose an Exploit. 2. Set the Target IP. 3. Choose a Payload (like a remote shell). 4. Type 'Exploit'. If the target is vulnerable, you're in!

quiz.question: In Metasploit, what is a 'Payload'?
options: The code that runs on the target system AFTER the exploit is successful
quiz.explanation: The exploit gets you through the door; the payload is what you actually want to DO once you're inside (like steal files).

cards.title: Guessing the Key
cards.body: A brute force attack tries every possible combination of letters and numbers to find a password. This is slow but guaranteed to work eventually if the password is simple.

cards.title: Dictionary Attacks
cards.body: A smarter version. It uses a list of millions of common passwords (like 'password123', 'admin', 'qwerty'). Tools like 'Hydra' or 'Burp Suite' do this automatically.

quiz.question: What is a 'Dictionary Attack'?
options: Trying every word in a list of common passwords
quiz.explanation: It's a targeted brute-force method that relies on the fact that most people use common, predictable passwords.

cards.title: Attacking the User
cards.body: If the server is too secure, hackers attack the person using it. Phishing emails trick users into downloading a 'Malicious Document' or entering their credentials into a fake site.

cards.title: Malicious Macros
cards.body: Hackers send an Excel file that says 'Enable Content to see Invoice'. Clicking that button runs a hidden script (Macro) that gives the hacker a backdoor to your PC.

quiz.question: Why do hackers use phishing instead of direct server attacks?
options: Human beings are often the 'weakest link' in security
quiz.explanation: Social engineering bypasses expensive firewalls by simply asking a human to let the attacker in.

cards.title: WPA2/WPA3 Cracking
cards.body: Hackers capture the 'Handshake'—the data sent when you connect to Wi-Fi. They then take that data home and use a GPU to guess the Wi-Fi password offline.

cards.title: Evil Twin Attack
cards.body: A hacker sets up a Wi-Fi network with the same name as the Cafe's Wi-Fi. Your phone connects automatically, and now the hacker can see every site you visit.

quiz.question: What is an 'Evil Twin' attack?
options: A fake Wi-Fi access point made to look legitimate
quiz.explanation: It's a social engineering attack where a rogue hotspot tricks users into connecting so their traffic can be intercepted.

cards.title: SQL Injection
cards.body: Attacking the database behind a website. By typing special code into a 'Login' box, a hacker can trick the database into showing them EVERY user's password.

cards.title: Prevention
cards.body: Developers must use 'Input Validation'. Never trust what a user types; always clean it before sending it to the database.

quiz.question: What is the primary target of an SQL Injection attack?
quiz.explanation: SQLi targets the database, allowing attackers to steal, change, or delete sensitive data stored by the web application.

modules.title: Maintaining Access & Clearing Tracks
modules.description: Staying inside and erasing the evidence.

cards.title: Permanent Entry
cards.body: Once a hacker is in, they don't want to exploit the system again. They install a 'Backdoor'—a hidden way to log in later without needing a password.

cards.title: Rootkits
cards.body: The ultimate backdoor. A rootkit hides deep inside the Operating System. It can hide its own files and processes so that even the Antivirus can't see it.

quiz.question: Why would a hacker install a backdoor?
options: To maintain access even if the original vulnerability is patched
quiz.explanation: Backdoors ensure the hacker doesn't lose their 'hard-earned' access if the system is updated or rebooted.

cards.title: Getting to Root
cards.body: Initial access is usually as a 'Low-level user' with no power. Privilege Escalation is the process of finding a second bug to become the 'Administrator' or 'Root'.

cards.title: Horizontal vs Vertical
cards.body: Vertical: Moving from User to Admin. Horizontal: Moving from one User to another User (e.g., hacking your coworker's account from your own).

quiz.question: What is 'Vertical' Privilege Escalation?
options: Hacking a computer on a different floor
Gaining a higher level of permission (like Admin)
quiz.explanation: Vertical escalation is about moving up the chain of command, from a restricted account to an all-powerful one.

cards.title: Hiding the Evidence
cards.body: To avoid getting caught, hackers must delete the 'Logs' that recorded their actions. They might delete 'auth.log' or specific entries that show their IP address.

cards.title: Modifying Timestamps
cards.body: Hackers use tools to change the 'Last Modified' date of files they changed to a date in the past, so the admin doesn't notice anything new.

quiz.question: Why do hackers delete system logs?
options: To hide the evidence of their activities from the administrator
quiz.explanation: Logs are the 'security camera footage' of a computer; deleting them is like erasing the tapes after a robbery.

cards.title: The Most Important Step
cards.body: For an Ethical Hacker, the 'Attack' is just the data collection phase. The real work is writing a report that explains HOW they got in and HOW the company can fix it.

cards.title: Executive Summary
cards.body: A good report has a technical section for IT staff and a 'High-level' summary for bosses that explains the business risk in simple terms.

quiz.question: What is the primary purpose of a penetration testing report?
options: To provide actionable advice on how to fix the security holes
To get the IT staff fired
quiz.explanation: The value of an ethical hacker is in helping the organization improve its defenses, not just finding holes.

cards.title: Stay Legal
cards.body: Without written permission (a 'Scope of Work'), hacking is illegal, even if you don't steal anything. 'I was just testing' is not a valid legal defense.

cards.title: Responsible Disclosure
cards.body: If you find a bug by accident, report it through the company's 'Bug Bounty' program or contact them privately. Never post a vulnerability online before it's fixed.

quiz.question: What is the most important thing to have before starting a penetration test?
quiz.explanation: Permission is what separates a professional ethical hacker from a common cybercriminal.
